topic: How UK cleaning-business owners migrate customer data off competitor software, and what Cadi's CustomerImport should do

insights.title: Squeegee — export format & quirks
insights.detail: Path: Reporting → 'All job data' → Download CSV (file is literally named 'All jobs.csv'). It's a JOBS export, not a customers export — one row per scheduled job, so customers with multiple services appear multiple times. Squeegee's own import template documents the conventions (which mirror what their export uses): Address must be populated; phone numbers must be stored as TEXT (leading 0s get stripped if Excel auto-formats); price as numeric; dates strictly YYYY-MM-DD. Squeegee deliberately keeps headers flexible ('no strict rules about what columns you need to include') and uses fuzzy/AI matching to its own fields, which means exports from different Squeegee tenants/versions can have slightly different headers. Common columns seen in the wild: Name, Address, Postcode, Balance, Phone, Email, Services, Frequency, Cost — plus per-job columns like Last Done, Next Due, Job Price, Round/Run. Quirk: a known bug imports every historical job as 'active', so a Squeegee export commonly contains hundreds of jobs that the owner stopped doing months ago.
insights.actionForCadi: Detect Squeegee by the filename 'All jobs.csv' OR the presence of (Services + Frequency + Cost) headers. Group rows by (Name + Postcode) into one customer with N services rather than N duplicate customers. Treat any job whose 'Next Due' or 'Last Done' is >12 months stale as 'inactive' and offer a checkbox 'Skip stale jobs (older than 12 months)' on the preview screen — directly addresses the 'every job imported as active' complaint. Force phone columns through String(...) (already done) and re-add a leading 0 if the value is 10 digits starting with 7 (UK mobile). Accept YYYY-MM-DD natively (already handled).

insights.title: CleanerPlanner — export format & quirks
insights.detail: Two separate exports: Customers.csv and Jobs.csv. getSoapy explicitly warns users not to upload both at the same time because it creates duplicates. The Jobs export is the rich one — columns include Cust Ref, Name, Address Line 1, Address Line 2, Town, Postcode, Mobile/Phone, Email, Round, Job Ref, Schedule (e.g. '4 weeks', 'monthly'), Price/Job Price, Balance, Due/Next Due, Status, Cancelled, Account Notes. One customer = many job rows (one per round/service). Dates are typically DD/MM/YYYY. Status field uses words like 'Active', 'Suspended', plus a separate Cancelled flag column. 'Quote' rows appear mixed in with real jobs and need filtering. CleanerPlanner also lets users add custom fields (e.g. their own 'Round' extension) so column names can drift between tenants.
insights.actionForCadi: Already well handled — isCleanerPlannerJobs() detects ('cust ref' + 'round' + 'job ref'). Current build dedups by Cust Ref and aggregates rounds. Two gaps: (1) the Customers.csv path isn't explicitly detected — add a separate detector for ('Cust Ref' + Name + Address but NO Job Ref) and warn 'Looks like you uploaded Customers.csv — upload Jobs.csv instead to get rounds & schedules' (mirrors getSoapy's UX). (2) Custom fields: when an unknown column appears alongside the canonical CP set, surface it on the map screen as 'Unmapped CP custom field — store as note?' rather than silently skipping.

insights.title: Aworka — export format & quirks
insights.detail: Same UX as CleanerPlanner: Jobs tab → Export → CSV. Aworka markets itself as 'Easy-Peasy' and is UK-focused on window/cleaning. CleanerPlanner's own migration help page treats Aworka exports as similar enough that they accept the same CSV. Columns observed in user discussions/migrations: Customer, Address, Postcode, Phone, Email, Round, Frequency (uses plain English: 'Every 4 weeks', 'Monthly', 'Fortnightly'), Price, Next Due, Last Done, Notes. Aworka stores recurring schedule as a human-readable string rather than a structured interval, so '6 weekly', 'six weekly', and '6 weeks' all appear.
insights.actionForCadi: parseFrequencyDays() already handles '4 weeks' and 'monthly' but will MISS 'six weekly', 'eight weekly' (worded numbers) and '4 wkly' / '4wk' (abbreviations). Add a word-to-number map (one/two/three/four/six/eight) and a regex for '\d+\s*wk' to cover Aworka exports. Also recognise 'every X weeks' prefix. Add explicit Aworka detection: presence of headers (Customer + Round + Next Due) without 'Cust Ref' suggests Aworka — auto-treat as a CleanerPlanner-shaped jobs file.

insights.title: Jobber — export format & quirks
insights.detail: Path: Clients → ⋯ → Export. Jobber's import template documents the column schema (since the CSV export mirrors importable fields). Address is broken into separate parts: Street 1, Street 2, City, State/Province, Zip/Postal Code, Country. Names are split: First Name, Last Name, Company Name (commercial clients use Company Name, residential use First/Last). Other columns: Email, Phone Number (often multiple — Mobile/Home/Work), Tags, Lead Source, Client Notes, custom fields. Jobber is North-American-first so 'State' is populated with English/Scottish counties (or blank) and 'Zip' contains the postcode. Jobs/visits are NOT in the clients export — a separate Import Jobs CSV exists with its own schema (Title, Description, Start Date, End Date, Client). Phone numbers often arrive in '+44 7…' international format with spaces.
insights.actionForCadi: Add keyword mappings: 'first name'→nameFirst, 'last name'→nameLast, 'company name'→nameCompany; combine into Cadi 'name' as 'First Last' or fall back to Company if First/Last empty. Map 'street 1'→addressLine1, 'street 2'→addressLine2, 'city'→town, 'state'/'province'→county, 'zip'/'zip code'/'postal code'→postcode. Strip '+44 ' → '0' on phone normalisation. Detect Jobber by the combination ('First Name' + 'Last Name' + 'Street 1'). Owners exporting clients-only from Jobber lose all visit history — surface a callout: 'Jobber's client export doesn't include visits. To bring in your schedule, also export Jobs from Schedule → Export and upload that next.'

insights.title: ServiceM8 — export format & quirks
insights.detail: Client list export is gated behind the paid 'Advanced Reporting Pack' add-on — many users won't have it and resort to copy-pasting the on-screen client list or a third-party tool. Where the add-on is enabled, the export CSV columns are: Client UUID, Name (the business/contact name), ABN, Billing Address (single concatenated string with newlines/commas), Billing Email, Phone, Fax, Mobile, Website, Active (1/0), Notes. ServiceM8 is Australian — addresses are concatenated into one field, dates are DD/MM/YYYY, phone fields often hold '04xx xxx xxx' (AUS mobile). 'ABN' will be blank for UK users. Jobs are exported separately and don't carry forward schedules — ServiceM8 doesn't really do recurring rounds the way UK window cleaners use them.
insights.actionForCadi: Add a one-field address parser: when the user maps a single column to 'address' (no separate town/postcode), split on commas and the last UK-postcode regex match — populate postcode from the matched substring, town from the penultimate part, addressLine1 from the rest. This single feature unlocks ServiceM8, QuickBooks (where address often arrives concatenated), and most paste-from-clipboard imports. Also recognise 'Active' column with 0/1 → accountStatus, and treat 'Client UUID' as customerReference.

insights.title: Cleaner Planner — export quirks beyond the main CSV
insights.detail: Same product as CleanerPlanner above (different spelling used by some users). The 'Jobs' export is the authoritative one. Additional things owners hit: the Schedule column can read 'one off' (Cadi already handles) but also 'AdHoc', 'on request' (which means no recurrence — should be stored as note, not as job); the Due column can be blank for newly-acquired customers; postcodes are sometimes stored without the space ('SW1A1AA'). Some tenants use a 'Round Day' column ('Mon', 'Tue') in addition to Round name. Cancelled customers carry an end date in a 'Cancelled' column rather than just a flag.
insights.actionForCadi: Add 'adhoc'/'on request'/'as required' → null frequency + push original text into notes. Normalise postcodes by inserting a space before the last 3 chars when the input has no space and matches UK pattern (^[A-Z]{1,2}\d[A-Z\d]?\d[A-Z]{2}$). Recognise 'Round Day' as a day-of-week scheduling hint and persist on customer/round for the Scheduler to honour. Treat 'Cancelled' column as a DATE (not boolean) — if a parseable date is present, set accountStatus='cancelled' and store the cancel date.

insights.title: Swept & CleanGuru — janitorial (commercial) exports
insights.detail: Both are commercial-cleaning (not window/domestic) so the data model is sites + buildings + contracts, not rounds. Swept's primary exports are time-tracking and payroll CSVs; its client export, where available, is just Site Name, Address, Manager Name, Manager Email, Manager Phone, Service Days. CleanGuru can export reports to Excel/CSV and the CRM holds 'billing information, security access codes and other data' but there is no documented standardised client CSV — exports come out of report builder so columns reflect whichever report the owner ran. CleanGuru pushes users toward QuickBooks export instead. Common pain point: contract value is monthly, not per-visit, and the existing software doesn't expose a 'price per visit' field — owners have to divide manually.
insights.actionForCadi: Two additions for commercial migrations: (1) accept 'Site Name' as an alias for 'name' and 'Manager Name'/'Site Contact' as a separate contactName field (currently lost); (2) when an imported row has a 'Monthly Value'/'Contract Value' column but no 'Price Per Visit', and a parseable 'Service Days' (e.g. 'Mon, Wed, Fri'), compute pricePerVisit = monthly / (visits/month) and store both. For Britannia-style commercial use the customer record already has site fields — wire the import to detectJobType()'s commercial path and skip 'exterior' tagging when manager/site fields are present.

insights.title: Cross-cutting pain points UK owners hit during migration
insights.detail: From window-cleaning forum threads and migration write-ups: (1) Duplicate explosion when the same customer has multiple rounds — one row per job becomes one customer per job. (2) Inactive/cancelled jobs imported as active — Squeegee's known bug means owners arrive at the new tool with hundreds of dead jobs cluttering the schedule. (3) Schedule strings the new tool doesn't understand (six weekly, every 5 wks, AdHoc, on request) silently fall back to no-schedule, so the customer never appears on the calendar — owners only notice weeks later when they realise they've stopped visiting. (4) Postcode/address mismatch when source split is different from target split. (5) Balances import as zero because the source CSV uses '£12.50' or '12.50 CR' (credit suffix) and the parser strips everything. (6) GDPR concern about leaving customer data on the old platform — owners ask for a 'confirm deleted from old tool' step. (7) No undo — if the import goes wrong, they don't know how to bulk-delete in the new tool and abandon migration.
insights.actionForCadi: In priority order: (a) add 'Skip jobs not done in 12 months' checkbox on preview (Squeegee fix); (b) extend parseFrequencyDays to cover worded numbers, 'every X weeks', 'X wkly', adhoc→null+note (Aworka/CP fix); (c) handle credit-suffix balances ('12.50 CR' → -12.50) and bracketed negatives ('(12.50)' → -12.50) in parseCurrency; (d) UK postcode normaliser (insert space, uppercase) on save; (e) single-field address splitter using postcode regex (ServiceM8/QuickBooks fix); (f) add an Undo Last Import action — store the import_batch_id on every customer/round/job created, surface a single 'Roll back this import' button on the Done screen and in Settings for 7 days; (g) at end of import, show a 'Tell your old tool to delete your data' card with a copy-paste GDPR request email pre-filled with the source vendor's support address.

insights.title: Specific code-level gaps in current CustomerImport.jsx
insights.detail: Read of /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/components/CustomerImport.jsx (lines 1-1182): the file already does the heavy lifting for CleanerPlanner Jobs (isCleanerPlannerJobs, buildCleanerPlannerData), Excel date handling, CleanerPlanner-style DD/MM/YYYY, '1st Jun 2026' parsing, currency stripping, and customer dedup by Cust Ref. KEYWORD_MAP covers most aliases. Specific holes: (1) no entries for 'first name'/'last name'/'company name' (Jobber); (2) no entries for 'street 1'/'street 2' (Jobber); (3) no 'site name'/'manager name' (Swept/commercial); (4) parseFrequencyDays misses worded numbers, 'wkly' abbrev, 'every X weeks' prefix, and 'adhoc'/'on request'; (5) parseCurrency drops 'CR' suffix and bracketed negatives without sign-flip; (6) no UK postcode normalisation; (7) no single-field address splitter; (8) no Jobber detector or Aworka detector — only CleanerPlanner has automatic format detection; (9) duplicate match is email-only — should fall back to (name+postcode) when email is blank, otherwise owners with phone-only customer lists get full dupes on re-import; (10) no 'rollback this import' affordance; (11) 'Skip stale jobs' option doesn't exist as a user-visible toggle (only in the generateJobDatesFromRound 12-month internal cap).
insights.actionForCadi: Concrete edits — extend KEYWORD_MAP with the Jobber/Swept aliases above; add nameFirst/nameLast/nameCompany Cadi fields and join in buildCustomers; extend parseFrequencyDays with the word/abbrev map; flip sign in parseCurrency on /CR\b/i or ^\(.*\)$; add normalisePostcode(); add splitConcatenatedAddress(); add isJobber/isAworka detectors mirroring isCleanerPlannerJobs; widen isDupe to (email) || (nameLower + postcodeLower); pass importBatchId through upsertCustomer/bulkInsertRounds/bulkCreateJobs so a rollback edge function can delete by batch; add a 'Skip jobs not done in 12 months' checkbox on StepPreview that filters entries before onImport.

topic: Migrating recurring schedules from competitor cleaning software into Cadi's Scheduler

insights.title: None of the major UK cleaning tools export a real RRULE — they all use frequency-in-weeks + last/next-due-date
insights.detail: Across Squeegee, Aworka, Cleaner Planner, Round Partner, SmartRound, WCP and getSoapy, the industry-standard model is a per-job 'frequency' (almost always an integer number of weeks: 1/2/4/6/8/12) plus a 'next due date' (and sometimes 'last done date'). Cleaner Planner is the canonical example — it exposes a 'Schedule next due from' setting (next-due is recalculated either from the originally-scheduled date or from the actual completion date). Squeegee uses the same model (frequency on rounds, auto-books the next clean on completion; supports patterns like 'each Wednesday of the second week'). Aworka is the same shape — Jobs tab > Export gives one CSV row per job/round with frequency + next-due. Jobber and ServiceM8 are the outliers: Jobber stores a real recurrence rule (FREQ/INTERVAL/BYDAY, iCal-style; report exports flattened visit rows with Visit Date + Frequency columns) and ServiceM8 has no real recurrence engine at all — it just clones the completed job at the same time/staff/interval after completion (a 'recur once completed' model with no stored rule). Practical consequence for Cadi: the importer should NOT require RRULE — it must accept the simpler {frequency_weeks, next_due_date, last_done_date} shape and synthesize an RRULE internally.
insights.actionForCadi: Make Scheduler's underlying job model store BOTH a parsed RRULE (for power) AND a denormalized {frequency_weeks, next_due_date} pair (for import/export compatibility with the UK round market). The chat-style import in onboarding should ask 'how often?' in weeks, not in cron syntax.

insights.title: Customer-job links are preserved by name+postcode, not by stable IDs — collision handling is the #1 migration risk
insights.detail: None of these tools expose their internal customer UUIDs in the CSV in a useful cross-system way. Squeegee's 'All jobs.csv' is a denormalized flat file (one row per job-visit with customer name, address, postcode repeated). Cleaner Planner offers separate Customers.csv and Jobs.csv but explicitly warns importers not to load both (getSoapy's docs confirm: import one OR the other to avoid duplicates). Jobber's recurring-jobs report is also denormalized — client info repeated per recurring job row. The de-facto join key the industry uses is normalized (name + postcode) or (address line 1 + postcode). Pitfalls confirmed from forum threads and migration vendor docs: (a) two customers at same postcode (flats, HMOs, Britannia-style commercial blocks) collide; (b) postcode formatting drift ('W8 4AA' vs 'W84AA') prevents dedup; (c) when both Customers and Jobs CSVs are loaded the job-side customer-name field creates a duplicate customer record. CleanerPlanner mitigates by handling migrations as a paid service (send file to support) — they do the messy name normalization manually.
insights.actionForCadi: Import pipeline must: (1) accept EITHER a combined denormalized CSV OR separate customer+job files; (2) normalize postcodes to uppercase no-space form before matching; (3) match customers on (normalized_postcode + fuzzy_name OR address_line_1); (4) show a pre-commit 'we found N likely-duplicate customers, merge?' review screen before writing rows. Do not auto-create customers from job rows without confirmation.

insights.title: Each vendor's quirk that the importer must explicitly handle
insights.detail: Squeegee: file is literally called 'All jobs.csv' (Reporting > All job data). One row per scheduled/completed visit, frequency lives on the round not the job — so the same customer can appear hundreds of times. Importer needs to collapse visit history into a single recurring rule. Aworka: simpler — Jobs tab > Export, one row per round; freq + next-visit fields. Cleaner Planner: exports Customers.csv AND Jobs.csv separately; jobs reference customers by name; has 'rounds' as a separate grouping concept that Cadi has no equivalent for (round name = drop region, not service). CleanGuru: US/janitorial-focused, exports via custom report builder to Excel/CSV (not a canonical schema — every user's file shape differs). Jobber: two relevant reports — Recurring Jobs Report (one row per recurring job with frequency) AND Visits Report (one row per visit instance); Cadi should accept the Recurring Jobs Report and ignore Visits (or use Visits only to backfill history). ServiceM8: requires Advanced Reporting Pack add-on to even get the Recurring Job List CSV — many users won't have it; falls back to Job List CSV which has no recurrence field, so importer must infer recurrence from repeated customer+service patterns or just ask the user. Cleaner Planner (the legacy WCP/George/Round Partner/SmartRound family): all use the weeks-interval model; CleanerPlanner already imports from them so its template is a reasonable reference shape.
insights.actionForCadi: Build vendor-specific parser presets (a dropdown: 'I'm coming from… Squeegee / Aworka / Cleaner Planner / Jobber / ServiceM8 / Other') that maps known column-header variants to Cadi's canonical fields. For ServiceM8 specifically, when no recurrence column exists, fall back to ServiceChat-style 'how often is each customer?' prompt rather than guessing.

insights.title: Concrete recommendation: Cadi Scheduler import schema (canonical columns)
insights.detail: Based on the union of competitor exports, the importer should accept these columns (case-insensitive, vendor-aware aliases): customer_name (aliases: Name, Client, Customer); address_line_1 (aliases: Address, Address1, Street); postcode (aliases: Postcode, Post Code, Zip); phone; email; service (aliases: Job, Service, Description, Round); price (aliases: Cost, Charge, Amount, Price); frequency_weeks (aliases: Frequency, Freq, Every, Interval, Schedule — accept '4', '4w', 'monthly'→4, 'fortnightly'→2, 'weekly'→1, '8-weekly'→8); next_due_date (aliases: Next Due, Next Visit, Next Date, Scheduled Date); last_done_date (aliases: Last Done, Last Visit, Completed); duration_mins (optional); assignee/crew (optional); notes; round_name (optional — Cadi can map to crew or a 'route' tag). For Jobber/iCal sources also accept rrule. Reject rows with no postcode AND no address_line_1 (cannot dedupe).
insights.actionForCadi: Add /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/lib/import/scheduleImport.js (next to the existing customerRoundsDb.js patterns) with: parseFrequency() that handles '4 weekly'/'monthly'/'fortnightly'/RRULE; normalizePostcode(); a vendor preset registry; and a dry-run preview that returns {customers_to_create, customers_to_merge, jobs_to_create, recurrence_rules_synthesized, warnings[]}. Wire it into the existing CustomerImport.jsx (already referenced in project memory at project_import_workflow.md) so the Schedule import lives alongside customer import.

insights.title: Pitfall: 'next due date' drift between systems silently breaks 4-week rounds
insights.detail: The biggest real-world pitfall (confirmed in forum threads about CP↔Squeegee moves and in Cleaner Planner's 'Schedule next due from' setting): the two recurrence policies — 'schedule from original due date' vs 'schedule from actual completion date' — produce divergent dates after one missed clean. If a customer was 'due 1 May, done 8 May' on 4-weekly: original-due policy schedules 29 May; completion-date policy schedules 5 Jun. Importing from a system using one policy into a system using the other shifts every recurring customer's next visit by days-to-weeks, silently. Britannia-scale (1500 staff, thousands of recurring contracts) makes this catastrophic — staff turn up on the wrong day. Squeegee defaults to completion-date; Cleaner Planner is user-configurable; Aworka behaves like completion-date.
insights.actionForCadi: Cadi Scheduler must (a) store BOTH original_due_date and last_done_date on every recurring job; (b) expose the 'schedule next from' policy as a per-customer setting (not global); (c) at import time, ask the user which policy their old system used and pre-fill accordingly; (d) on the post-import review screen, show the first 3 projected next-due dates per customer so the user can sanity-check before committing.

insights.title: Pitfall: Cadi's current Scheduler has no recurrence model at all — jobs are flat date rows
insights.detail: Reading src/pages/Scheduler.jsx (lines 82-122 demo data, and the DayView/WeekView job shape throughout): every job is a flat {id, customer, type, service, postcode, startHour, durationHrs, price, status, assignee, assignees, date} record. There is no frequency field, no next_due, no parent recurring-job ID, no RRULE. The Scheduler treats each visit as a one-off. listAllRounds() is imported from customerRoundsDb but rounds appear to be a separate concept (the 'Rounds' view tab at line 343). This means today, even if the importer parsed a recurrence rule perfectly, there's nowhere to store it — the imported recurring round would land as N concrete date rows (the next M visits expanded), losing the rule. Every subsequent visit after the expansion window would have to be regenerated manually.
insights.actionForCadi: Before building the importer, add a recurring_jobs (or job_templates) table at the DB layer with: customer_id, service, frequency_weeks, rrule (nullable), next_due_date, last_done_date, schedule_policy ('original'|'completion'), price, duration_mins, default_assignee. The existing jobs table becomes 'visit instances' with optional recurring_job_id FK. The Scheduler render path needs a generator that projects future visits from recurring_jobs into the visible window on demand (don't pre-materialize years ahead). Then the importer writes recurring_jobs rows (the source of truth) instead of fanning out visit instances.

topic: Competitor service-menu builders + recommendations to make Cadi's bespoke menu easier

insights.title: Jobber — Products & Services list: solid but spreadsheet-feeling
insights.detail: Jobber's Products & Services lives in Settings as a flat searchable list. Each line item has name, description, unit cost, unit price, taxable toggle, optional max-quantity for online booking. Strengths: very fast to add line items to quotes/jobs once built (~40% faster invoicing claim), CSV import/export for bulk updates and across-the-board price changes, integrates with Job Costing if unit cost is filled. Weaknesses: it's a generic line-item list, not a cleaning-tuned menu — no concept of per-bed pricing, frequency, inclusions/exclusions or 'site visit required'. Cleaning owners end up encoding logic in names ('3 bed weekly clean — kitchen+bath') and it doesn't power instant quoting on a website. CSV import is unforgiving on column names and formats.
insights.actionForCadi: Cadi already wins on cleaning-native fields (pricing_matrix, frequency_*, inclusions/exclusions). Keep that lead. Match Jobber on bulk operations: ship a 'bulk edit pricing' view (raise all prices by X%, duplicate across categories) and a CSV round-trip — the Squeegee/Jobber migrators will expect it.

insights.title: ServiceM8 — Services & Materials with bundles; partner-led setup is the tell
insights.detail: ServiceM8 lets you build 'services' and 'materials' and combine them into preset 'bundles' (one customer-facing description hiding labour + materials lines). It can auto-import emailed supplier price-list updates. Strength: bundles are powerful for repeatable jobs. Weakness: the setup is notoriously fiddly — ServiceM8 actively steers users to paid Partner consultants for setup/training, which is a direct admission that the menu builder is too clunky for a non-technical cleaner to onboard alone. No instant-quote consumer-facing layer.
insights.actionForCadi: This is the single biggest moat. ServiceM8 needing paid partners to configure a service menu is exactly what Cadi's conversational + AI-assisted builder kills. Lean into 'no consultant needed — talk to Cadi for 5 minutes' in marketing copy on /features.

insights.title: Housecall Pro — Price Book + Pricing Forms: best-in-class for trades, weaker for cleaning nuance
insights.detail: Housecall Pro ships a free pre-populated Price Book with industry templates (home cleaning, carpet, window/exterior) — services come with pre-written descriptions, you just fill in prices. They also have 'Pricing Forms' — a flat-rate form for e.g. weekly home clean that works regardless of property size — and integrate Profit Rhino for HVAC/plumbing flat-rate. CSV import has a column-mapping wizard. Strengths: lowest cold-start friction (template + descriptions written for you), pricing forms feed instant online booking. Weaknesses: $59/mo+ entry, US-centric (no £/postcode logic), and cleaning templates are shallow vs. plumbing/HVAC. The flat-rate model doesn't map cleanly to UK 'per bed' or 'per hour with minimum'.
insights.actionForCadi: Steal the template idea hard. Cadi should ship a sub-industry template library (domestic / commercial / carpet / window / Airbnb turnover / end of tenancy) where each template comes pre-loaded with name + inclusions + exclusions + pricing_type recommendation + UK-typical price band shown as a hint ('most 2-bed weekly cleans in the UK are £45–£70'). User just confirms or tweaks. This collapses the entire ServiceChat flow to 1–2 taps for the 80% case.

insights.title: Squeegee — UK window-cleaning native, but pricing lives on the customer record
insights.detail: Squeegee is UK-built, mobile-first, designed for window cleaning rounds. It charges per-user (£19–£33/user/mo). Pricing philosophy is fundamentally different: there isn't a true 'service menu' — pricing is set per-customer on the round (you charge Mrs Smith £15 every 4 weeks). Strengths: mobile-first round management, fast prospect-to-job. Weaknesses: no reusable price book/menu — every new customer is re-priced from scratch. Doesn't scale beyond solo/small window crews. No instant online quoting from a structured menu.
insights.actionForCadi: Squeegee migrators are the easiest converts because they have no menu to port — they just need to articulate their typical prices once. Build a 'coming from Squeegee?' onboarding lane: 'You probably price each customer individually. Let's just capture your typical rates so Front Desk can quote new enquiries.' Conversational builder is already perfect for this.

insights.title: CleanGuru / CleanBid — commercial-janitorial bid engine, dated UI
insights.detail: CleanGuru is commercial-only: feed it square footage + carpet/restroom/floor type and it spits out a recommended bid using their algorithm (production rates × labour cost + materials + margin). Strengths: takes the guess-work out of commercial bidding for the long-tail of contracts. Weaknesses: '90s UI', no residential, no consumer-facing instant quote, no bespoke menu in the modern sense — it's a calculator, not a menu.
insights.actionForCadi: For Britannia-scale commercial users, the Commercial Survey build already covers this ground better. The takeaway is: cleaners think in production rates (£/m² or m²/hour) for commercial — make sure the per_sqm pricing type's helper copy explicitly references this language.

insights.title: Recommendation 1 — Sub-industry template library (highest leverage, lowest build cost)
insights.detail: Add a 'Start from a template' affordance to Services.jsx alongside the existing Add a service / Catalogue picker. Templates are sub-industry packs: Domestic regular, Domestic deep/EoT, Airbnb turnover, Carpet cleaning, Window cleaning (UK), Gutter & exterior, Office contract, Post-construction. Each pack pre-fills name + category + pricing_type + suggested pricing_matrix labels + inclusions + exclusions + frequency flags. Show a small 'UK typical price' band as a hint (data-driven later — for v1, hand-curate). One tap installs 3–5 services with sensible defaults; user just tweaks the £ figures. This is what Housecall Pro's pre-populated Price Book does — but Cadi can do it with cleaning-native fields they don't have.
insights.actionForCadi: Add /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/lib/serviceTemplates.js exporting an array of {pack, services[]}. Wire a 'Templates' tab into CatalogueModal in Services.jsx (lines 406–536). Reuse seedServicesFromOnboarding pattern.

insights.title: Recommendation 2 — Photo of a price list → menu (the killer feature)
insights.detail: Single biggest delighter. Add an 'Upload a photo of your current price list' button in the Services page empty state and in ServiceChat's welcome step. Flow: user snaps a photo of their handwritten or printed price list (every cleaner has one — on a clipboard, in a Word doc, on their website, in a leaflet). Send to Claude vision (Sonnet, structured output) with a prompt that returns Service[] matching the existing toRecord shape. Show extracted services in a confirmation grid where each row is editable inline; user taps 'Save all'. Same flow handles: paste-from-website (URL fetch + Claude), paste-from-text, PDF upload (extract text, feed to Claude), CSV from Jobber/Squeegee export. One backend edge function 'extract-service-menu' that takes {source_type, content} and returns normalised services.
insights.actionForCadi: Deploy a Supabase edge function extract-service-menu using Anthropic vision. Add a dropzone+camera component above the chat in ServiceChat.jsx. The toRecord function (lines 277–307) is already the target schema — vision just needs to be prompted to that JSON shape. This is the feature to lead the /features page demo with.

insights.title: Recommendation 3 — Voice input on the chat (huge for van-based owners)
insights.detail: Cleaning owners are in vans, in customers' kitchens, between jobs — typing is the wrong input. Add a mic button to the ChatInput component. Use the browser SpeechRecognition API for free (works offline-ish, no cost) with a fallback to a Supabase edge function calling Whisper for accuracy on iOS Safari (which has flaky SpeechRecognition). The existing parsePricing/parseDuration/parseIncEx regex layer already handles natural speech patterns ('eighteen pounds an hour with a three hour minimum') — voice just feeds those parsers. Show a waveform while recording so it doesn't feel like a black hole.
insights.actionForCadi: Edit ChatInput in /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/components/ServiceChat.jsx lines 358–380 to add a mic button. New hook useVoiceInput() wrapping webkitSpeechRecognition with edge-function Whisper fallback. No schema changes needed — feeds existing handlers.

insights.title: Recommendation 4 — Paste-from-website (URL ingestion)
insights.detail: Most established cleaners have a website with their prices on it. Add a 'I already have a website with my prices' option in onboarding and in the Services empty state. Input is a URL; backend fetches the page, strips boilerplate, sends body to Claude with a service-extraction prompt, returns services to confirm. Handles WordPress, Squarespace, Wix — wherever their current prices live. This is a near-zero-friction migration path from any competitor or any website builder.
insights.actionForCadi: Same edge function as Recommendation 2 (extract-service-menu) with source_type='url'. Add to the welcome step in ServiceChat.jsx (line 651–671) as a third option alongside 'Let's go' and 'Start somewhere else': 'I'll grab my prices off my website'.

insights.title: Recommendation 5 — Bulk operations & cross-service tools (parity + power)
insights.detail: Once a user has 20+ services (Britannia-scale), single-service editing is too slow. Add: (a) 'Raise all prices by X%' action on the Services page header (Jobber parity); (b) duplicate-across-category (clone Residential weekly clean → Commercial weekly clean with one tap); (c) CSV export of full menu (for users who want a backup or to share with their accountant); (d) inline edit of price in the card (don't make them open chat for a £45 → £50 change — that's overkill).
insights.actionForCadi: Add a 'Bulk actions' menu in the Services.jsx header (around line 1246–1259). Inline-edit price by making the pricing summary on ServiceCard click-to-edit. Wire updateService for the single field.

insights.title: Recommendation 6 — Sharpen the conversational flow itself
insights.detail: Two specific frictions in current ServiceChat.jsx: (a) the chat is *only* surfaced for 'first_setup' and 'edit/add' — it doesn't run when the user pastes a price list, which is where AI shines most; (b) the Quick mode finishes after duration but doesn't ever surface a 'this matches a template — want to copy the template's inclusions?' suggestion. Once a name + category + pricing are captured, do a quick semantic match against the template library and offer 'Most weekly cleans include: hoovering, dusting, kitchens, bathrooms. Use these?' as a one-tap yes. This combines Rec 1 + the chat without forcing the user to choose between them upfront.
insights.actionForCadi: After handlePricing in ServiceChat.jsx (~line 708), add a template-suggestion step before going to DURATION. New helper suggestTemplateMatch(name, category) returning template inclusions for one-tap accept. Keep skip as a button.

insights.title: Where competitors are clunky — confirms Cadi's positioning
insights.detail: Recurring complaints across G2/Capterra/forum threads: (1) ServiceM8 requires paid partners to set up — admission of UX failure on the menu builder; (2) CSV imports across all of them break on column-name mismatches and currency formats — users abandon halfway; (3) Housecall Pro's template descriptions are written in US English ('drapes', 'baseboards') and need rewriting for UK customers; (4) Jobber has no concept of inclusions/exclusions so quotes over-promise; (5) Squeegee has no menu at all — fine for solo window cleaners, breaks at any scale; (6) None of them accept a photo of an existing price list as input. The 'photo → menu' feature alone is differentiated on the market.
insights.actionForCadi: Marketing angle for /features page: 'Snap a photo of your current price list. We'll build your menu in 30 seconds.' Lead with this. Demo video should show a clipboard photo → populated service cards.

topic: Wow-moment SaaS patterns mapped to Cadi's Schedule, Customers, and Services tabs

insights.title: Pattern 1 — Instant-value seeded first run (Notion templates, Linear demo workspace)
insights.detail: Best-in-class tools never show an empty state on first load. Notion drops in a template; Linear seeds a demo project with realistic issues you can poke at; Superhuman pre-loads your inbox before the welcome screen ends. The wow is 'it already knows what I do'. Cadi already has buildDemoJobs() in Scheduler.jsx line 83 but it only fires in demo mode. The opportunity is to seed the real schedule with 3-5 ghost jobs derived from the onboarding seed (customer types, typical day, average price) and let the user accept/edit/dismiss each — the schedule looks 'full' within 30 seconds of finishing onboarding.
insights.actionForCadi: Extend buildDemoJobs() in /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Scheduler.jsx into a buildSeedJobs(onboardingProfile) helper, and render ghost cards with an accept-or-dismiss affordance the first time the tab is opened.

insights.title: Pattern 2 — Command palette as the universal verb surface (Linear Cmd-K, Notion, Attio, Pylon)
insights.detail: Cmd-K is the single shortcut that means 'do anything'. Linear's palette navigates, creates issues, assigns, switches projects, all from one input. The wow is keyboard-first speed and the sense the product compresses to one key. Cadi has AskCadi.jsx (396 lines) as a chat surface but no synchronous palette. A palette that resolves 'book Mrs Smith Tuesday 10am', 'find Hendersons', 'add deep clean service £180' against local data would feel magical.
insights.actionForCadi: Create /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/components/CommandPalette.jsx mounted in App.jsx, with handlers that delegate into Scheduler.jsx (createJob), Customers.jsx (findCustomer/openCustomer) and Services.jsx (addService). Bind Cmd/Ctrl-K.

insights.title: Pattern 3 — Magic paste / smart import (Linear paste-issues-from-spreadsheet, Notion paste-table, Cal.com paste-availability)
insights.detail: You paste raw text or a CSV chunk and the product parses it into structured rows with inferred columns. Linear specifically detects a pasted markdown table and offers 'import as issues'. The wow is zero schema mapping. Cadi already has CustomerImport.jsx but it is a form, not a paste-anywhere surface.
insights.actionForCadi: Add a global onPaste listener in /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Customers.jsx that, when the clipboard looks like rows of name/address/phone, opens a confirm-import drawer pre-mapped via Haiku — same for pasting a price list into Services.jsx.

insights.title: Pattern 4 — AI-assisted setup that asks instead of forms (Superhuman onboarder, Pylon Sage, Attio AI fields)
insights.detail: Conversational setup turns a 30-field form into a 6-turn chat. Cadi already nailed this with Onboarding.jsx and ServiceChat.jsx. The next frontier is a CustomerChat — a natural-language path for adding a customer ('the Smiths at 12 Oak Lane, fortnightly, £45, prefer Tuesdays') that maps to fields without typing into them.
insights.actionForCadi: Mirror ServiceChat.jsx as /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/components/CustomerChat.jsx and wire it into Customers.jsx as the primary add flow, demoting AddCustomerModal (line 2048) to 'classic form'.

insights.title: Pattern 5 — Optimistic UI with undo toast (Linear, Superhuman archive)
insights.detail: Actions resolve instantly client-side, with a 5-second undo toast at the bottom. No spinners, no 'are you sure' dialogs. Superhuman's archive feels weightless because undo is right there. Cadi has Toast() in Services.jsx line 1061 but no undo affordance, and Customers.jsx uses an ArchiveButton with confirm (line 1272) — a friction point.
insights.actionForCadi: Promote Toast in Services.jsx into a shared /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/components/ui/UndoToast.jsx and replace the confirm in Customers.jsx ArchiveButton and Scheduler.jsx job-cancel flows with optimistic remove + 5s undo.

insights.title: Pattern 6 — Live presence and 'someone else is here' indicators (Notion avatars, Linear cursors)
insights.detail: Even in solo tools, seeing teammate avatars on the same view creates a sense of liveness. Cadi has TeamMembersUI.jsx and a Britannia-scale 1500-staff context — showing which cleaner is currently looking at their own job card on Scheduler would be a wow for an owner who sees 'Jane just opened her Tuesday route'.
insights.actionForCadi: Add a presence channel via Supabase Realtime in /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Scheduler.jsx, rendering small staff avatars on the day column they are currently viewing in the StaffDashboard.

insights.title: Pattern 7 — Inline natural-language scheduling (Cal.com, Linear /due, Superhuman snippets)
insights.detail: Typing 'next Tuesday 10am' resolves to a real date inline as you type. Linear lets you type '/due fri' inside an issue. The wow is the parser feels telepathic. Cadi's Scheduler has fixed grid hours (GRID_HOURS at line 126) and presumably a date-picker on job create — perfect place for a chrono-style input.
insights.actionForCadi: Add a single-line 'quick book' input at the top of /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Scheduler.jsx that parses '<customer> <day> <time> <service>' into a job via chrono-node + fuzzy customer match against the customers table.

insights.title: Pattern 8 — Smart suggestions surfaced in-context (Attio AI fields, Notion Q&A, Pylon next-best-action)
insights.detail: Modern CRMs don't wait for you to ask — they surface 'this customer hasn't been visited in 6 weeks, send a check-in?' as a card in the customer record. Cadi has generateSuggestions(customer) at Customers.jsx line 52 but it appears tied to MessageComposer. Surface those suggestions as a persistent strip at the top of CustomerDetail.
insights.actionForCadi: In /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Customers.jsx CustomerDetail (line 1307), add a 'Cadi suggests' carousel above the fold that renders 2-3 cards from generateSuggestions, each one-tap actionable (Send, Book, Dismiss).

insights.title: Pattern 9 — Drag-and-drop as the primary verb (Linear board, Notion kanban, Pylon triage)
insights.detail: Dragging a card between columns to change state is faster than any menu. Cadi's Scheduler.jsx has STATUS_STYLES (line 68) and STATUS_LABELS (line 75) but the day grid appears time-based. Adding a parallel 'today's pipeline' lane (To-do / On the way / On site / Done) where staff or owner can drag jobs would feel native.
insights.actionForCadi: Add a TodayPipeline view toggle inside /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Scheduler.jsx using react-beautiful-dnd or @dnd-kit, sharing the layoutDayJobs data (line 263).

insights.title: Pattern 10 — Keyboard-only navigation with j/k and ? cheatsheet (Superhuman, Linear, Pylon)
insights.detail: Power users live on the keyboard. j/k to move between rows, e to edit, x to archive, ? to show all shortcuts. Cadi's CustomerRow (line 520) and ServiceCard (line 923) are pure mouse targets today. Adding a focused-row model and a discoverable '?' overlay would unlock 10x speed for repeat users.
insights.actionForCadi: Add /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/hooks/useListKeyboardNav.js and apply it to the customer list in Customers.jsx (line 2300, CustomerTab) and the service grid in Services.jsx (line 1075). Ship a ShortcutsOverlay.jsx bound to ?.

insights.title: Pattern 11 — Recurring-as-a-rule, not as N copies (Cal.com event types, Linear cycles)
insights.detail: Cal.com models a weekly slot as a rule, not 52 events. The wow is editing one definition and watching every future instance update live. Cadi's Customers.jsx JOB_TYPES (line 32) and FREQ_OPTIONS in Services.jsx (line 69) suggest frequencies exist but jobs are probably materialised. Showing a 'see next 12 visits' preview that re-renders as you tweak the rule is the magic move.
insights.actionForCadi: Inside /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Customers.jsx CustomerDetail's job-booking flow, add a live-preview strip that shows the next 12 generated visit dates as the user edits frequency/day — same component reusable on Services.jsx for templated recurring services.

insights.title: Pattern 12 — Activity stream / 'what changed' digest (Linear inbox, Notion updates, Attio timeline)
insights.detail: A unified 'since you last looked' feed makes the product feel alive. Cadi has Inbox.jsx already, but a per-customer timeline ('booked / paid / messaged / rescheduled') inside CustomerDetail would close the loop and reduce 'what's the status' questions. Also pairs with the AnnualReview.jsx narrative.
insights.actionForCadi: Add a Timeline tab inside /Users/mackiescleaning/cleaning business blueprints/cleaning-blueprints/src/pages/Customers.jsx CustomerDetail (line 1307) reading from a customer_events table, and a daily-digest card on the Dashboard summarising deltas across all three tabs.
